questions.text: I find that I am drawn to roles and situations where my capacity for clear thinking during emotional intensity is needed.
I find that emotional upheaval in my environment doesn't destabilize me internally.
I feel that having contingency plans is essential because things rarely go as expected.
I experience moments of awe so strongly that they bring me to a standstill.
I pick up on when a group collectively decides to pretend something did not happen.
I hold the view that suffering, when recognized, creates a moral imperative that should be reflected in my actions, priorities, and choices.
I feel restless when no one is taking charge of a situation.
I believe that remaining analytically engaged while others are emotionally overwhelmed is a contribution that often goes unrecognized.
I am energized by the process of building a decision together.
I am wired to flag what seems broken and let the group decide how to address it.
I believe that tracking my own financial growth is a satisfying and important practice.
I notice when a person clenches or unclenches their hands in rhythm with the emotional intensity of a conversation.
I believe that the felt weight of another person's hardship is a form of moral evidence that deserves serious consideration.
I hold the view that unused capacity in my life represents a missed opportunity to serve.
I can look back on difficult periods without re-experiencing the emotional intensity.
I think that the most equitable treatment of people's needs comes from those who can separate the emotional from the factual.
I rarely notice when someone is performing busyness to avoid a conversation.
I have changed my plans because I became aware that someone needed help, even when it was inconvenient.
I value speed and momentum over precision in the early stages of any effort.
I feel that spending lavishly on my own experiences is a worthwhile use of money.
I prefer to help execute a shared vision rather than set one alone.
I feel the emotional atmosphere of a room the moment I walk in, and it lands in my body immediately.
I can detect insincerity in someone's tone of voice even over the phone.
I walk into meetings ready to engage with whatever comes up, without advance preparation.
I find that writing everything down makes me feel constrained rather than empowered.
I think of authority as something to be exercised, not just held.
I identify the single most important task each day and make sure it gets done first.
I tend to miss social cues that seem obvious to others.
I tend to miss when a conversational pause is an invitation for me to share something personal.
I rarely feel that my emotions are too much for me to handle.
I feel that having a single decision-maker leads to clearer outcomes.
I find deep contentment in giving away things I worked hard to earn.
I believe that preparation is what separates success from failure in most endeavors.
I find that returning home after time away produces a surge of feeling so strong it catches me off guard.
I hold the view that my personal library, workspace, and equipment should reflect my standards.
I hold the view that I should always be looking for ways to lighten someone else's burden.
